title: The Psychology of Colour
description: For questions 17 – 24, read the text below. Use the word given in capitals at the end of some of the lines to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: There is now (0) scientific evidence that colour can significantly affect our moods and behaviour. Researchers have found that red tends to increase (17) excitement and stimulate appetite, which is why it is commonly used in fast-food (18) advertising. Blue, on the other hand, is often associated with feelings of (19) calmness and trust.||Businesses use colour (20) strategically to influence consumer (21) perception and encourage (22) loyalty. Interior designers also consider colour carefully, as it can affect (23) productivity in the workplace and a person's overall sense of (24) wellbeing. Understanding colour psychology has become an important tool in both marketing and design.

example.text: There is now (0) SCIENTIFIC evidence that colour can significantly affect our moods and behaviour. (SCIENCE)

questions.explanation: The noun 'excitement' is formed from 'excite' by adding the suffix '-ment'.
The noun/gerund 'advertising' is formed from 'advertise' by adding the suffix '-ing'.
The noun 'calmness' is formed from 'calm' by adding the suffix '-ness'.
The adverb 'strategically' is formed from 'strategy' via 'strategic' + the suffix '-ally'.
The noun 'perception' is formed from 'perceive' by replacing '-ceive' with '-ception'.
The noun 'loyalty' is formed from 'loyal' by adding the suffix '-ty'.
The noun 'productivity' is formed from 'produce' via 'product' + '-ivity'.
The compound noun 'wellbeing' is formed by combining 'well' and 'being'.
